Distance-based learning with manydist and tidymodels

Author

Alfonso Iodice D’Enza 

set.seed(123)
rm(list = ls())
devtools::load_all("../")
OK: All competitor packages are already installed.

Competitor packages loaded successfully:
 [1] "proxy"        "parallelDist" "Rfast"        "klaR"         "arules"      
 [6] "cluster"      "FD"           "StatMatch"    "gower"        "clustMixType"
# library(manydist)
library(tidyverse)
library(tidymodels)
library(palmerpenguins)

1 Introduction

The manydist package provides a flexible framework for distance-based learning with continuous, categorical, and mixed-type predictors. Its core function mdist() computes mixed-variable dissimilarities under a range of presets and custom specifications, while the MDist class stores both the resulting distance matrix and the associated metadata. The package also integrates with the tidymodels ecosystem through a recipe step, step_mdist(), and a custom distance-based nearest-neighbour model, nearest_neighbor_dist().

This vignette illustrates the main functionalities of the package:

  1. computing mixed-type distances with mdist();
  2. assessing variable influence with lovo_mdist();
  3. comparing diagnostics across methods with compare_lovo_mdist();
  4. creating distance-based representations with step_mdist();
  5. fitting and tuning a KNN model with nearest_neighbor_dist() and tune_grid().

The focus is on classification of penguin species, but the same ideas apply to other supervised or unsupervised tasks that rely on pairwise distances.

2 Data

We start from the penguins data and remove rows with missing values.

penguins_clean <- penguins |>
  drop_na()

penguins_clean |>
  count(species)
# A tibble: 3 × 2
  species       n
  <fct>     <int>
1 Adelie      146
2 Chinstrap    68
3 Gentoo      119

3 Computing mixed-type distances with mdist()

The main entry point of the package is mdist(), which computes a mixed-variable dissimilarity according to a chosen preset or custom specification.

d_obj_all <- mdist(
  x = penguins_clean |> select(-species),
  preset = "unbiased_dependent"
)

d_obj_all
MDist object
  preset : unbiased_dependent 
  number of observations : 333 
  number of continuous variables   : 5 
  number of categorical variables   : 2 
  parameters:
    - continuous distance method: manhattan
    - categorical distance method: tvd
    - scaling for continuous vars: pc_scores
    - commensurability adjustment: TRUE

The resulting object stores the distance matrix together with the distance specification.

class(d_obj_all)
[1] "MDist" "R6"   
dim(as.matrix(d_obj_all$distance))
[1] 333 333

4 Variable diagnostics with lovo_mdist()

The function lovo_mdist() provides a leave-one-variable-out diagnostic. For each predictor, the function recomputes the distance after removing that variable and compares the resulting structure with the full-distance solution.

lovo_res <- lovo_mdist(
  x = penguins_clean |> select(-species),
  preset = "unbiased_dependent"
)

summary(lovo_res)
Summary of MDistLOVO
  preset : unbiased_dependent 
  dims   : 2 
  n_obs  : 333 

MAD:
  range [1.0000, 1.2957], mean 1.0446
Alienation Coefficient (AC):
  range [0.0363, 0.5269], mean 0.1999

Top by MAD:
# A tibble: 5 × 2
  variable          mad_importance
  <chr>                      <dbl>
1 sex                         1.30
2 bill_length_mm              1.01
3 body_mass_g                 1.00
4 bill_depth_mm               1.00
5 flipper_length_mm           1.00

Top by AC:
# A tibble: 5 × 2
  variable       ac_importance
  <chr>                  <dbl>
1 sex                   0.527 
2 island                0.378 
3 bill_depth_mm         0.190 
4 body_mass_g           0.122 
5 bill_length_mm        0.0933

A graphical summary is also available through autoplot().

autoplot(lovo_res, "mad_normalized")

5 Comparing distance specifications with compare_lovo_mdist()

The function compare_lovo_mdist() extends the LOVO diagnostic to multiple distance specifications. This makes it possible to compare how variable contributions change across alternative mixed-type distances.

In the example below, we compare:

  • "gower", a standard mixed-type distance;
  • "unbiased_dependent", an association-aware distance that enforces commensurability;
  • a custom specification based on Manhattan distance for numeric variables and Hennig–Liao distance for categorical variables.
lovo_cmp <- compare_lovo_mdist(
  x = penguins_clean |> select(-species),
  methods = list(
    gower = list(
      preset = "gower"
    ),
    u_dep = list(
      preset = "unbiased_dependent"
    ),
    custom = list(
      distance_cont = "manhattan",
      distance_cat  = "HL",
      commensurable = FALSE
    )
  )
)

lovo_cmp
MDistLOVOCompare object
  methods: gower, u_dep, custom 
  dims   : 2 
  n_obs  : 333 
  rows   : 21 

Top variables by MAD within each method:
# A tibble: 9 × 4
  method variable          mad_importance ac_importance
  <fct>  <chr>                      <dbl>         <dbl>
1 gower  island                    0.0730       0.240  
2 gower  sex                       0.0697       0.378  
3 gower  year                      0.0481       0.0501 
4 u_dep  sex                       1.30         0.527  
5 u_dep  bill_length_mm            1.01         0.0933 
6 u_dep  body_mass_g               1.00         0.122  
7 custom body_mass_g             913.           0.416  
8 custom flipper_length_mm        15.9          0.00908
9 custom bill_length_mm            6.27         0.00994
summary(lovo_cmp)
Summary of MDistLOVOCompare
  methods: gower, u_dep, custom 
  dims   : 2 
  n_obs  : 333 

# A tibble: 3 × 7
  method mad_min  mad_max mad_mean   ac_min ac_max ac_mean
  <fct>    <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>  <dbl>   <dbl>
1 gower   0.0261   0.0730   0.0431 0.0501    0.378  0.141 
2 u_dep   1.000    1.30     1.04   0.0363    0.527  0.200 
3 custom  0.883  913.     134.     0.000653  0.416  0.0627
autoplot(lovo_cmp, metric = "mad_normalized")

6 Distance-based KNN in base R

We now move to a simple predictive setting and create a stratified train/test split.

set.seed(123)
peng_split <- initial_split(penguins_clean, strata = species)
peng_train <- training(peng_split)
peng_test  <- testing(peng_split)

dim(peng_train)
[1] 249   8
dim(peng_test)
[1] 84  8

We separate predictors and outcome:

x_train <- peng_train |>
  select(-species)
x_test  <- peng_test |>
  select(-species)
y_train <- peng_train$species
y_test  <- peng_test$species

We compute train-to-test distances with mdist().

d_obj <- mdist(
  x = x_train,
  new_data = x_test,
  preset = "unbiased_dependent"
)

d_obj
MDist object
  preset : unbiased_dependent 
  number of training observations : 249 
  number of test observations     : 84 
  number of continuous variables   : 5 
  number of categorical variables   : 2 
  parameters:
    - continuous distance method: manhattan
    - categorical distance method: tvd
    - scaling for continuous vars: pc_scores
    - commensurability adjustment: TRUE
distance_matrix <- as.matrix(d_obj$distance)
dim(distance_matrix)
[1]  84 249

These distances can be used directly in a minimal KNN implementation.

n_neighbours <- 5

nn_idx <- apply(distance_matrix, 1, function(d) order(d)[1:n_neighbours])

pred_base <- apply(nn_idx, 2, function(idx) {
  names(which.max(table(y_train[idx])))
})

mean(pred_base == y_test)
[1] 0.9761905

7 Distance-based representations with step_mdist()

To integrate with tidymodels, manydist provides the recipe step step_mdist(), which transforms the predictor set into a distance-based representation.

peng_rec <- recipe(species ~ ., data = peng_train) |>
  step_mdist(all_predictors(), preset = "unbiased_dependent")

peng_rec
Step: mdist
  role:        predictor
  trained:     FALSE

  preset:      unbiased_dependent
  (arguments handled internally by preset)
prepped_rec <- peng_rec |>
  prep()
train_dist <- bake(prepped_rec, new_data = peng_train)
test_dist  <- bake(prepped_rec, new_data = peng_test)

8 Distance-based KNN with nearest_neighbor_dist()

The package also provides a custom parsnip model, nearest_neighbor_dist(), with a "precomputed" engine.

knn_spec <- nearest_neighbor_dist(
  mode = "classification",
  neighbors = 5
) |>
  set_engine("precomputed")

knn_spec
nearest neighbor dist Model Specification (classification)

Main Arguments:
  neighbors = 5

Computational engine: precomputed

We can combine the recipe and the model in a workflow.

peng_wf <- workflow() |>
  add_recipe(peng_rec) |>
  add_model(knn_spec)

peng_wf
══ Workflow ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
Preprocessor: Recipe
Model: nearest_neighbor_dist()

── Preprocessor ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
1 Recipe Step

• step_mdist()

── Model ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
nearest neighbor dist Model Specification (classification)

Main Arguments:
  neighbors = 5

Computational engine: precomputed 
peng_fit <- peng_wf |>
  fit(data = peng_train)

Predictions and performance evaluation are then straightforward.

peng_pred <- predict(peng_fit, new_data = peng_test, type = "prob") |>
  bind_cols(
    predict(peng_fit, new_data = peng_test, type = "class"),
    peng_test |> select(species)
  )

peng_pred |>
  head()
# A tibble: 6 × 5
  .pred_Adelie .pred_Chinstrap .pred_Gentoo .pred_class species
         <dbl>           <dbl>        <dbl> <fct>       <fct>  
1          1               0              0 Adelie      Adelie 
2          0.8             0.2            0 Adelie      Adelie 
3          1               0              0 Adelie      Adelie 
4          1               0              0 Adelie      Adelie 
5          1               0              0 Adelie      Adelie 
6          1               0              0 Adelie      Adelie 
peng_pred |>
  accuracy(truth = species, estimate = .pred_class)
# A tibble: 1 × 3
  .metric  .estimator .estimate
  <chr>    <chr>          <dbl>
1 accuracy multiclass     0.976

9 Tuning the number of neighbors

Because nearest_neighbor_dist() is registered as a parsnip model, its hyperparameters can be tuned using tune_grid().

knn_tune_spec <- nearest_neighbor_dist(
  mode = "classification",
  neighbors = tune()
) |>
  set_engine("precomputed")

knn_tune_spec
nearest neighbor dist Model Specification (classification)

Main Arguments:
  neighbors = tune()

Computational engine: precomputed 
peng_wf_tune <- workflow() |>
  add_recipe(peng_rec) |>
  add_model(knn_tune_spec)
set.seed(123)
peng_folds <- vfold_cv(peng_train, v = 5, strata = species)

k_grid <- tibble(neighbors = seq(3, 15, by = 2))
k_grid
# A tibble: 7 × 1
  neighbors
      <dbl>
1         3
2         5
3         7
4         9
5        11
6        13
7        15
peng_metrics <- metric_set(accuracy)

tuned_knn <- tune_grid(
  peng_wf_tune,
  resamples = peng_folds,
  grid = k_grid,
  metrics = peng_metrics
)
collect_metrics(tuned_knn)
# A tibble: 7 × 7
  neighbors .metric  .estimator  mean     n std_err .config        
      <dbl> <chr>    <chr>      <dbl> <int>   <dbl> <chr>          
1         3 accuracy multiclass 0.984     5 0.00774 pre0_mod1_post0
2         5 accuracy multiclass 0.988     5 0.00494 pre0_mod2_post0
3         7 accuracy multiclass 0.984     5 0.00403 pre0_mod3_post0
4         9 accuracy multiclass 0.984     5 0.00398 pre0_mod4_post0
5        11 accuracy multiclass 0.984     5 0.00398 pre0_mod5_post0
6        13 accuracy multiclass 0.984     5 0.00398 pre0_mod6_post0
7        15 accuracy multiclass 0.984     5 0.00398 pre0_mod7_post0
show_best(tuned_knn, metric = "accuracy")
# A tibble: 5 × 7
  neighbors .metric  .estimator  mean     n std_err .config        
      <dbl> <chr>    <chr>      <dbl> <int>   <dbl> <chr>          
1         5 accuracy multiclass 0.988     5 0.00494 pre0_mod2_post0
2         9 accuracy multiclass 0.984     5 0.00398 pre0_mod4_post0
3        11 accuracy multiclass 0.984     5 0.00398 pre0_mod5_post0
4        13 accuracy multiclass 0.984     5 0.00398 pre0_mod6_post0
5        15 accuracy multiclass 0.984     5 0.00398 pre0_mod7_post0
autoplot(tuned_knn)

After selecting the best value of k, the workflow can be finalized and re-fitted on the training set.

best_k <- select_best(tuned_knn, metric = "accuracy")

final_wf <- peng_wf_tune |>
  finalize_workflow(best_k)

final_fit <- final_wf |>
  fit(data = peng_train)

final_pred <- predict(final_fit, new_data = peng_test, type = "class") |>
  bind_cols(peng_test |> select(species))

final_pred |>
  accuracy(truth = species, estimate = .pred_class)
# A tibble: 1 × 3
  .metric  .estimator .estimate
  <chr>    <chr>          <dbl>
1 accuracy multiclass     0.976

10 Summary

In this vignette we showed how manydist can be used for distance-based learning on mixed data through:

  • mixed-type distance computation with mdist();
  • variable diagnostics with lovo_mdist();
  • method comparison with compare_lovo_mdist();
  • recipe-based representations with step_mdist();
  • distance-based KNN modeling and tuning with nearest_neighbor_dist().
